coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of red-emitting carbon dots for bioimaging, sensing, and antibacterial applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Jiao
Li, Ting
Lin, Chengzhang
Hou, Yongxing
Cheng, Shuanghuai
Gao, Bingmiao
coredata.dc:description: It is a highly desirable and formidable challenge to synthesize carbon dots with long-wavelength emission using green synthesis. In this work, we explored red-emitting carbon dots (rCDs) via a hydrothermal strategy and their multifunctional application for bioimaging in vivo/vitro, curcumin sensing, and antibacterial materials. As-prepared rCDs were water-soluble and monodispersed with an average diameter of 2.34 nm. Significantly, these rCDs exhibited low toxicity and outstanding biocompatibility, which was consistent with the excellent bioimaging performance in living cells, zebrafish, and nude mice, providing them a promising prospect for clinical applications. Meanwhile, the obtained rCDs were also used as a fluorescent probe for sensitive detection of curcumin in a wide linear range of 0.03–135.73 μM with a limit of detection of 29.37 nM. Furthermore, quaternized rCDs were designed and used as antibacterial material with minimum inhibitory concentrations against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli of 0.15 mg/mL and 0.5 mg/mL, respectively, which advanced the development of novel antibacterial agents and broadened the applications of red-emitting CDs. Therefore, this work provided multifunctional CDs with red emission for use in the fields of biological imaging, fluorescence sensing, and antibacterial materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red emission
Multifunctional carbon dots
Bioimaging
Curcumin
Antimicrobial

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Acute toxicity experiments in vitro and in vivo
Cytotoxicity and acute toxicity test in vivo

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Hainan Provincial Key Point Research and Invention Program (no. ZDYF2022SHFZ309), Natural Science Foundation of Hainan Province For High-level Talents Project (821RC568) and a Special scientific research project of the Hainan academician innovation platform (no. YSPTZX202132). Moreover, the authors are grateful for the support and assistance in terms of zebrafish, instruments and facilities provided by the Public Research Center of Hainan Medical University, as well as the help of Shuanghuai Cheng in the operation of transmission electron microscopy for ultrastructure photography.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of rCDs (inset: the optical images of rCDs solution under daylight and UV light), (B) emission spectra of rCDs excited at excitation wavelengths from 450 nm to 550 nm), (C) CIE chromaticity coordinates of rCDs.

Characterization of rCDs. (A) TEM image (inset: the particle size distribution), (B) HRTEM image, (C) XRD pattern, (D) Raman spectrum, (E) FT-IR spectrum, (F) Full XPS spectrum. High-resolution XPS of (G) C1s, (H) N1s, and (I) O1s with identification of peaks by curve fitting.

The cytotoxicity on A549 cells (A), acute toxicity in mice (B), reproductive toxicity on zebrafish embryo (C) and the representative images of its embryo development (D) treated with various concentrations of rCDs.
Fluorescent images of rCDs in A549 living cells.
The bioimaging of rCDs in zebrafish (A) and nude mouse (B).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of rCDs in the presence of various curcumin concentrations (from a to y: 0.00–50.11 μg/mL), inset: the standard curve of the concentration of curcumin versus F 0/F; (B) The selectivity of rCDs to various metal ions and other interfering substances.

The fluorescence spectra (A), fluorescence decay curves (B), and FT-IR spectra (C) of rCDs, curcumin, and rCDs–Cur. (D) A schematic description of the preparation of rCDs and the quenching mechanism for detecting curcumin.

Pictures of LB agar plates and bacterial survival of S. aureus (A) and E. coli (B) after incubation with different concentrations of Q-rCDs. Differences compared with the control were assessed using ANOVA. *p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001, **** p < 0.0001.
Test results of curcumin in the actual sample (n = 3).
Comparison of different detection methods used for curcumin detection.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of red-emitting carbon dots for bioimaging, sensing, and antibacterial applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center of Tropical Medicine Innovation and Transformation of Ministry of Education, Hainan Provincial Key Laboratory of Research and Development on Tropical Herbs, International Joint Research Center of Human-machine Intelligent Collaborative for Tumor Precision Diagnosis and Treatment of Hainan Province, School of Pharmacy, Hainan Medical University, Haikou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center of Tropical Medicine Innovation and Transformation of Ministry of Education
Hainan Provincial Key Laboratory of Research and Development on Tropical Herbs
International Joint Research Center of Human-machine Intelligent Collaborative for Tumor Precision Diagnosis and Treatment of Hainan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Engineering Research Center of Tropical Medicine Innovation and Transformation of Ministry of Education, Hainan Provincial Key Laboratory of Research and Development on Tropical Herbs, International Joint Research Center of Human-machine Intelligent Collaborative for Tumor Precision Diagnosis and Treatment of Hainan Province, School of Pharmacy, Hainan Medical University, Haikou, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is a highly desirable and formidable challenge to synthesize carbon dots with long-wavelength emission using green synthesis. In this work, we explored red-emitting carbon dots (rCDs) via a hydrothermal strategy and their multifunctional application for bioimaging in vivo/vitro, curcumin sensing, and antibacterial materials. As-prepared rCDs were water-soluble and monodispersed with an average diameter of 2.34 nm. Significantly, these rCDs exhibited low toxicity and outstanding biocompatibility, which was consistent with the excellent bioimaging performance in living cells, zebrafish, and nude mice, providing them a promising prospect for clinical applications. Meanwhile, the obtained rCDs were also used as a fluorescent probe for sensitive detection of curcumin in a wide linear range of 0.03–135.73 μM with a limit of detection of 29.37 nM. Furthermore, quaternized rCDs were designed and used as antibacterial material with minimum inhibitory concentrations against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli of 0.15 mg/mL and 0.5 mg/mL, respectively, which advanced the development of novel antibacterial agents and broadened the applications of red-emitting CDs. Therefore, this work provided multifunctional CDs with red emission for use in the fields of biological imaging, fluorescence sensing, and antibacterial materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a rising star in the field of fluorescent nanoparticles, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted tremendous attention due to their low cost, easy preparation, favorable optical properties, good water solubility, and superior biocompatibility [1]. Because of these numerous merits, CDs have been widely employed in various applications including bioimaging, sensing, antimicrobial, drug delivery, and optoelectronics [2,3]. However, the emission of conventional CDs is mostly produced in the blue-to-green light region, which restricts their application in the biomedical field due to the interruption of autofluorescence in biosystems [4]. Consequently, the development of long-wavelength emission CDs is indispensable and desirable [5]. Unlike blue/green light-emissive CDs, the synthetic routes for red-emitting CDs are limited and frequently involve the utilization of poisonous precursors or organic solvents [6], such as phenylenediamine, formamide [7], acetone, and dimethyl formamide [8], which are considered as efficient factors contributing to redshift fluorescence [9]. However, these poisonous precursors or organic solvents are detrimental to human health and the environment. Therefore, there is a high demand to explore facile and green preparation strategies for multifunction red/NIR-emitting CDs (λem > 600 nm).

Curcumin, a natural pigment, is widely used in food, medicine, cosmetics, and other fields because of its unparalleled bioactivities [10]. As a food additive, curcumin has been applied in various foods, including seasonings, candies, beverages, and dairy products. Studies have shown that excessive curcumin may result in cell necrosis due to the disorder of intercellular ATP [11,12]. For this reason, numerous analytical detection techniques for curcumin have been developed, including spectrophotometry, capillary electrophoresis, thin-layer chromatography, and high-performance liquid chromatography [13,14]. Although these methods show high accuracy and selectivity, most of them demand expensive equipment, highly skilled experimenters, and cumbersome and time-consuming procedures [15]. Hence, it is essential to establish a rapid, low cost as well as high efficiency analytical system for detecting curcumin at low concentrations [16]. Over the past few decades, antibiotics have been used to kill microbes and prevent wound infection [17]. However, overuse and misuse of antibiotics can accelerate environmental pollution as well as the evolution of multidrug-resistant bacteria [18]. With the rapid development of nanotechnology, various nanomaterials have been considered as an alternative approach to address this dilemma [19–21]. Among them, the drawbacks (e.g., expensive precursors, synthetic complexity, and cytotoxicity) of metal nanostructure and semiconducting quantum dots have unfortunately impeded their practical application in the medical field [22,23]. Therefore, biocompatible CDs stand-out in the field of antibacterial research on account of their unparalleled merits [24–26]. So far, only a few red/NIR-emitting CDs with antimicrobial activity have been reported and some of them require an external light source, which inspires researchers to employ antibacterial red-carbon dots as a potential antibacterial biomaterial to expand their application fields [27,28].

With the aforementioned information in mind, red-emission multifunctional CDs (rCDs) were manufactured through a one-step hydrothermal method using neutral red (NR) and l-arginine (l-Arg) as precursors and utilized for in vitro/vivo bioimaging, curcumin (Cur) sensing, and antibacterial material. The obtained rCDs showed high fluorescence quantum yield (QY), low cytotoxicity, and excellent biocompatibility, supplying a fluorescent probe for biolabeling A549 cells, zebrafish, and mice. In addition, the rCDs were successfully applied as a label-free nanoprobe for Cur. The linear range was 0.03–135.73 μM and the limit of determination (LOD) was 29.37 nM. Furthermore, the rCDs were quaternized easily by dodecyl dimethyl betaine (Q-rCDs) and exhibited significant antibacterial activity with the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) against Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) of 0.15 mg/mL and that against Escherichia coli (E. coli) of 0.5 mg/mL.

NR, l-Arg, 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC•HCl), rhodamine B, 2-(N-morpholino)ethanesulfonic acid (MES), and N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide (sulfo-NHS) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. Curcumin was purchased from Bide Pharmatech Co., Ltd. Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM) was purchased from Wuhan Pricella Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Phosphate-buffered saline (pH 7.4), Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK8), and Penicillin-Streptomycin solution (100 × ) were purchased from Beijing Lanjie Ke Technology Co., Ltd. S. aureus (GDMCC801366) and E. coli (GDMCC1.737) were provided by Guangdong Microbial Culture Collection Center. Luria Broth (LB) agar and LB Broth (with glucose) were purchased from Guangdong Huankai Microbial Sci. Tech. Co., Ltd. Wild-type AB strain zebrafish were obtained from the Hangzhou Hunter Biotech Co., Ltd. Milli-Q ultrapure water was used in all experiments.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded on an F-7100 spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescence lifetimes were detected and calculated by FluoroMax+ (Horiba, Ltd., Sunnyvale, CA, USA), UV–Vis absorption spectra were acquired by a V650 UV–Vis Spectrophotometer (Persee Analytics, Pek, CHN). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a Nicolet IS5 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained with JEM-F200 (Japan Electron Optics Laboratory, Tokyo, Japan) and HT7800 (Hitachi, Ltd.) transmission electron microscopes. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) images were obtained on a D8QUESTX X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Bruker Corporation, Billerica, MA, USA). Raman spectra were obtained using an inViaQontor Raman microscope (Renishaw Corporation, Mountain View, CA, USA). The fluorescence images of zebrafish and A549 cells were captured using a fluorescent microscope (Olympus, Tokyo, Japan; MVX10 + DP74) and a confocal laser scanning microscope (Olympus, FV3000), respectively.

The rCDs were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method. Briefly, l-Arg (500 mg) and NR (5 mg) were completely dissolved in 15 mL of ultrapure water using a vortex mixer. Then, the solution was transferred into a 30 mL stainless steel autoclave reactor lined with p-polyphenylene and maintained at 160℃ for 12 h. The solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove black solid deposits and then the supernatant was further purified using a 3000 Da micro separator. The ultrafiltrate was the purified rCDs solution, then the red powder of rCDs was obtained by freeze drying.

Using rhodamine B (QY = 0.74, η = 1.36) dissolved in ethanol as a standard [29,30], the relative QY of rCDs was calculated. The formula for calculating relative QY is as follows:
Φ =
Φ R
F /
F R
A R
/ A
η /
η R
2
× 100 % ,
where Ф is QY, F indicates the fluorescence area, A indicates the absorbance, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and R is the standard substance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Acute toxicity experiments in vitro and in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: The CCK8 method was applied to assess the cytotoxicity of rCDs. Briefly, A549 cells were seeded in 96-well plates and cultured at 37 °C and 5.0 % CO2 for 4 h. Then, the culture medium was replaced with a medium containing rCDs with a series of concentrations (0–1 mg/mL). After 24 h of culture at 37 °C, the culture medium was discarded, and then CCK-8 reagent was added to each well for 2 h. Afterward, the absorbance of each well was measured at 450 nm using a microplate reader.

Kunming mice (Swiss mice) of both sexes (18–22 g) were used to evaluate the acute toxicity in vivo of prepared rCDs. These mice were injected intraperitoneally with different concentrations of rCDs (5, 10, 15 mg/kg). Mice were treated with physiological saline as the control group. Three replicates of four individuals were used for every concentration tested. The lethal effect was checked 48 h after injection.

The assessment of embryo development was also performed using healthy zebrafish embryos. Briefly, 0 h-post-fertilization (hpf) embryos were collected from spawning adults, rinsed with system water and immediately exposed to the 4 mL E3 metdium contained different concentrations of rCDs (0, 50, 100 and 250 μg/mL). The embryos were placed in 6-well plates and ten embryos were placed in each plate with three replicates for each control and treatment group. Media were replaced every 24 h with fresh E3 metdium contained respective rCDs concentrations. Zebrafish were observed and photographed at each measurement time point (0, 24, 48, 72, 96, 120 hpf) for morphological analyse. The survival and hatching rates were calculated manually.

A549 human non-small cell lung cancer cells were seeded in confocal-specific glass culture dishes and cultured with DMEM medium at 37 °C, 5 % CO2 for 24 h. Then, the culture medium of A549 cells was replaced with 1 mg/mL rCDs solutions and incubated for 0.5 h. Finally, fluorescent imaging was performed by CLSM after being washed with a sterile PBS buffer. A549 cells treated without rCDs were used as the control group.

Zebrafish and Balb/c-nu mice were used for the purpose of evaluating the practical applicability of rCDs as fluorescent probes in a living organism. Five-day-old zebrafish were fed with 1 mg/mL rCDs at room temperature for 30 min and washed with PBS buffer. The zebrafish imaging was achieved using a stereo fluorescence microscope. The bioimaging of rCDs was further evaluated in Balb/c-nu mice by intraperitoneal injection (5 mg/kg). The fluorescence signals of mice were recorded on an IVIS Lumina II system (Revvity, Waltham, MA, USA. Zebrafish and mice were treated without rCDs as the control groups.

As-prepared rCDs powder was dissolved with 0.01 M PBS solution (pH = 9) and the concentration of rCDs was 500 μg/mL. Then, 200.44 mg of curcumin was dissolve in 100 mL ethanol to obtain a stock with a concentration of 2.00 mg/mL. Different volume of stock solution was transfer into 5 mL volumetric flask and diluted by ethanol to produce a serial of curcumin solution with different concentration. 100 μL of these curcumin solutions were separately added to 3.9 mL PBS solution (0.01 M, pH 9) containing rCDs (500 μg/mL). The fluorescence emission spectra were obtained after 1 min.

Some nanomaterials used for fluorescent detection of curcumin in real samples have been reported earlier [16,31]. Briefly, ginger rhizomes were purchased from local supermarkets (Haikou, China). The rhizomes were washed to remove its surface residues and chopped into small pieces. Then 200 g chopped rhizomes were added into juice extractor to obtain the ginger paste. Then added 100 mL ethanol and extracted with ultrasonic for 30 min. The mixed solution was then filtered and centrifuged at 13,000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was collected for further use. 0.0252 g of curcumin was dissolve in 10 mL obtained GJ supernatant to obtain a stock with a concentration of 2.52 mg/mL. Then the 0.4, 0.8 and 1.2 mL of stock solution were diluted with 5 mL of ginger juice to obtain the sample solution. Then 100 μL of these solutions were separately added to 3.9 mL PBS solution (0.01 M, pH 9) containing rCDs (500 μg/mL), and the final concentrations of curcumin were 5.04, 10.08 and 15.12 μg/mL respectively. Finally, the fluorescence emission spectra were obtained (Ex = 500 nm).

According to previous literature [32], CDs can be quaternized using dodecyl dimethyl betaine. Briefly, 282.47 mg EDC•HCl, 319.97 mg sulfo-NHS, and 300 μL dodecyl dimethyl betaine (30 %) were dissolved in 1 mL MES solution (0.1 M, pH = 6.0) for 30 min. Then, 4 mL PBS solution containing rCDs (50 mg/mL) was added to the mixed solution for 12 h in an oscillating device. After dialysis (MWCO: 500) and freeze-drying, Q-rCDs were obtained for further characterization.

The antibacterial ability of Q-rCDs was assessed using Gram-positive bacteria and Gram-negative bacteria like S. aureus and E. coli, respectively. The bacterial strains were cultured in LB medium at 37 °C with shaking at 200 rpm. After centrifugation and washing with sterile saline, bacteria were adjusted to 1.5 × 108 CFU·mL−1 via a turbidity comparator. Subsequently, different doses of Q-rCDs solution were added to bacteria suspensions (1.5 × 106). After reacting for 3 h, the mixed solution was diluted with culture medium and then poured into the culture dish. The number of bacterial colonies was counted after 24 h of incubation at 37 °C. Control groups without Q-rCDs were treated in the same way.

To achieve rCDs with desirable optical properties, the experimental conditions including reaction time, temperature, and the usage amount of l-Arg and NR were optimized via an L9(34) orthogonal experiment. Rhodamine B was used as a reference to measure the QY of rCDs under different reaction conditions. As shown in Fig. S1, the optimal reaction time was 12 h, the optimal temperature was 160 °C, and the optimal usages of l-Arg and NR were 500 mg and 5 mg, respectively. Thus, it was selected to synthesize rCDs further using l-Arg and NR as raw materials at a temperature of 160 °C with heating for 12 h. Rhodamine B was used as a reference, and the QY of prepared rCDs reached 22.9 % (Table S1), which was higher than other CDs synthesized with NR as raw material [4,33–35].

Synthesized rCDs having good water solubility were likely based on preparation by a hydrothermal process with surface functional groups. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of the rCD solution were located at 500 and 624 nm, respectively. Meanwhile, the rCDs solution emitted red fluorescence under UV light of 365 nm (the inset pictures in Fig. 1A). The 3-D fluorescent spectra (Fig. 2 B) indicated that the emission wavelength of rCDs exhibited a redshift with the increase in excitation wavelength from 450 to 550 nm, which suggested that rCDs had excitation-wavelength-dependent properties. In Fig. S2, the fluorescence lifetime of prepared rCDs was 1.59 ns (Fig. S2). Moreover, the fluorescence property of rCDs was further investigated using the chromaticity diagram (CIE), and the CIE coordinate was calculated as (0.6380, 0.3617), which proved a promising application of rCDs as a fluorescent probe in biological systems (Fig. 1C).

As depicted in Fig. 2A, the TEM image of rCDs demonstrated their excellent dispersibility in water and uniform nanoparticles with a diameter of 2.34 ± 0.76 nm (n > 200). A clear lattice fringe with a spacing of 0.23 nm was revealed by high resolution TEM (HRTEM) presented in the inset of Fig. 2B, representing the (100) facet of graphite [36]. The presence of graphitic structure in rCDs was also demonstrated by a diffraction peak located at 2θ = 21.9° in Fig. 2C, attributed to a facet of the carbon atoms in graphite, which is the characteristic XRD diffraction peak of amorphous carbon materials [37]. The coexistence of disordered area (sp3 domain) and carbon network (sp2 domain) in the carbon material was further validated by the Raman spectrum in Fig. 2D, where the two peaks at 1313 and 1437 cm−1 were assigned to the D band and G band, respectively. The intensity ratio was calculated (I D/I G = 0.95), which demonstrated a large proportion of defects in the prepared rCDs [38].

The surface functional groups and structural compositions of rCDs were further analyzed using FT-IR, 1H NMR, and XPS spectra. In the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2E), the peaks around 3270, 2950, 1660, and 1100 cm−1 coincided with the stretching vibration of –OH/–NH, C–H, C = O and C–N, respectively, whereas the peaks around 1460 cm−1 coincided with the bending vibration of C–H [39]. The results validated the existence of hydrophilic groups (amino and hydroxyl) on the surface of rCDs, contributing to the excellent water solubility of rCDs (>50 mg/mL in water), which would benefit its bioapplications. Besides, the surface structure of rCDs can be further elucidated using NMR spectroscopy [40]. Depending on the 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. S3), the peaks around 3.43, 2.83, 1.98, and 1.52 ppm were attributed to the hydrogens of l-Arg, whereas several weak peaks located at 8.25, 7.37, 7.21, and 6.93 ppm could be ascribed to phenazine protons in NR, indicating that rCDs have been assembled successfully by the coupling of l-Arg and NR. The peaks associated with NR exhibited weak intensity due to the amount of NR to synthesize rCDs being notably tiny. The full-scale XPS pattern (Fig. 2F) indicated three signals at 288.08 eV (C1s), 401.08 eV (N1s), and 533.08 eV (O1s), confirming that rCDs were comprised of C, N, and O, in the atomic proportions of C: 61.62 %, N: 22.26 %, and O: 16.12 %. Significantly, the nitrogen content is up to 22.26 % in this as-prepared rCDs, much higher than that of other reported rCDs [36,40,41]. In the high resolution XPS, the C1s spectrum (Fig. 2G) could be decomposed into four peaks at 284.34, 287.86, 285.12, and 286.25 eV, which corresponded to C–C/C = C, C = O, C–N, and C–O, respectively. Bands 285.12 eV(C–N) might be assigned to –NHCO–, which was due to the reaction between carboxyl and primary amino during production of rCDs. These demonstrated the chemical modification realized via amidation [42]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2H) contained three peaks located at 399.60, 398.76, and 400.45 eV, which were assigned to C–N–C, C = N, and C2–N–H bands, respectively. The C1s and N1s spectra clearly indicated the successful doping of nitrogen atoms in rCDs, which had been previously proved that red emitting CDs could be achieved by increasing the nitrogen content on account of the new surface state with a lower energy level formed on the surfaces of carbon dots [36]. Peak 398.76 eV was probably arisen from C = N of NR, which also indicated the incorporation of NR into the core of rCDs. Two distinct peaks at 530.62 and 532.27 eV were deconvoluted in the O1s spectrum (Fig. 2I), which could be attributed to C = O and C–O–O/C–OH. Notably high surface oxidation degree could also contribute to the red-shift of CDs. All these XPS analyses were consistent with the FT-IR results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and acute toxicity test in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: The CCK8 test was performed to evaluate the cytotoxicity of rCDs toward A549 cells. As indicated in Fig. 3 A, the cell viability did not significantly change with rCDs from 0 to 1.0 mg/mL. Moreover, results in the acute toxicity experiment also indicated the rCDs showed nontoxicity to mice within 48 h even in the high concentration group (15 mg/kg), as indicated in Fig. 3B. All mice did not have abnormal behaviors in acute toxicity experiments and were still alive after the experiment. Furthermore, the zebrafish embryos were used to evaluate the effect of rCDs on embryo development (Fig. 3C and Fig. 3D). The survival and hatching rates of treated zebrafish embryos in all concentrations and those in the control group were not significantly different (Fig. 3C). The red–orange color of zebrafish embryo and larvae had been enhanced with the increasement of rCDs concentration, which demonstrated the absorption of rCDs into the zebrafish (Fig. 3D). Remarkably, although the embryo mortality increased in a concentration-dependent manner, the growth retardation between the treatment groups and the control showed no obvious change at 72 hpf. In conclusion, it was demonstrated that the prepared rCDs showed favorable promise for biological application in vitro and in vivo on account of their superior biocompatibility and ultralow cytotoxicity.

For living cell imaging, the A549 cells were incubated with 1 mg/mL rCDs for 0.5 h, and bright red fluorescence was emitted inside the cells as compared with the control group (Fig. 4 ), indicating rCDs could be internalized in the cytoplasm of A549 cells rather than adsorbing on membrane surfaces.

In addition to in vitro cell imaging, in vivo zebrafish and mice imaging were performed to prove their practicability further in biological applications. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the zebrafish treated with rCDs (1 mg/mL) could emit intense green and red fluorescence, when they were stimulated by different wavelength lasers. It was demonstrated that rCDs mainly localized in the belly of zebrafish. In addition, Fig. 5B revealed strong red fluorescence in the left ventral cavity of nude mice by intraperitoneal injection with 100 μL of rCDs (5 mg/kg). Moreover, all mice survived without any sign of acute toxicological responses after the experiment, demonstrating the admirable biocompatibility of the prepared rCDs.

To establish a highly sensitive and stable method for the detection of curcumin, several factors including the system pH, the concentration of rCDs, temperature, and reaction time were optimized. As Fig. S4A shows, the values of fluorescent quenching rate represented by (F 0 − F)/F 0 were significantly enhanced in alkaline conditions compared with acidic conditions, indicating that the pH condition played a determining role in the test system. Subsequently, the influences of concentration of rCDs, temperature, and reaction time on the quenching efficiency were also studied (Fig. S4B–D). In conclusion, the quenching efficiency reached a maximum when the curcumin was detected using 0.5 mg/mL rCDs at pH = 9.0 and room temperature within 5 min. Using the optimal reaction conditions, different concentrations of curcumin were detected using rCDs. As illustrated in Fig. 6 A, the fluorescence intensity of rCDs gradually decreased with the increase in curcumin concentration. Excellent linearity between the Napierian logarithm of F 0/F and the concentration of curcumin in the range of 0.012–50.11 μg/mL (0.033–135.73 μM) with the coefficient of determination (R 2) of 0.9984 (inset in Fig. 6A). The LOD was 10.82 ng/mL (29.37 nM) and the average recoveries were between 97.65 % and 101.90 % (Table S3), verifying the feasibility and veracity of the analytical method for curcumin analysis. Furthermore, a selective experiment of rCDs for curcumin detection was performed, which used 12 kinds of representative metal ions and seven kinds of small-molecule compounds as competitive coexisting substances. In Fig. 6B, the fluorescence of rCDs treated with 25 μg/mL (0.07 mM) curcumin was obviously quenched, whereas the interfering substances could rarely quench the fluorescence intensity of rCDs even in a high concentration (1 mM), indicating that the prepared rCDs exhibited favorable selectivity and sensitivity for curcumin.

The UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra, fluorescence lifetime, and FT-IR spectra of the reaction system were obtained to investigate the quenching mechanism. The fluorescence quenching mechanisms of CDs can be divided into five kinds: static quenching, dynamic quenching, energy transfer, photoinduced electron transfer, and inner filter effect (IFE) according to the previous literature [11,43]. Being different from static or dynamic quenching, IFE (also called apparent quenching) usually refers to the excitation or emission spectra of CDs overlapped with the absorption of a quencher, which could not change the fluorescence lifetime of CDs and generate new formations. In Fig. S6, the absorption spectrum of the mixed solution was conformable to the superposition of the curves of rCDs and curcumin, which exhibited no additional peak nor blueshift or redshift. It demonstrated no exciplex formation between rCDs and curcumin [12]. The emission peak of rCDs also did not generate a bathochromic shift or hypsochromic shift before and after adding curcumin (Fig. 7 A), suggesting that rCDs did not generate aggregation or decomposition in the reaction system. In addition, the excitation spectra of rCDs showed a partial overlapping with the absorption band of curcumin (Fig. S6 and Fig. 1A). The spectral overlapping revealed that fluorescence change might be related to IFE between rCDs and curcumin, due to curcumin absorbing or trapping the emission light from rCDs [16]. As depicted in Fig. 7B, the addition of curcumin made only a minor change to the fluorescence lifetime of rCDs, which also verified that the possible quenching mechanism is IFE rather than energy and charge transfer. These results are consistent with relevant studies [13,33]. Furthermore, FT-IR spectra showed changes in the surface functional groups of rCDs and curcumin (Fig. 7C). In the spectra of curcumin, the absorption bands appearing at 3500 and 3390 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of N–H and O–H, respectively, whereas the band located at 1630 cm−1 indicated the presence of the C = O stretching vibration. Moreover, the bands located at 1140 cm−1 and 1070 cm−1 corresponded to the C–O and N–H stretching vibrations, respectively. Compared with the spectra of rCDs–Cur, these characteristic peaks had no evident change. In conclusion, the quenching mechanism of rCDs and curcumin could be explained by IFE, as depicted in Fig. 7D.

To expand the application of rCDs, they can be easily modified with dodecyl dimethyl betaine (BS12) against bacteria. The characterization of quaternized rCDs (Q-rCDs) is depicted in detail in the supplementary information. As shown in Fig. S7A, the Q-rCDs showed a spherical-like structure with an average diameter of about 5.31 ± 1.13 nm, which was a little larger than that of the bare rCDs. Fig. S7B exhibits a broad diffraction peak located at 20.2° in the XRD pattern, which is similar to the result for bare rCDs. The FT-IR spectra revealed that the absorption curve of Q-rCDs was conformable to the superposition of curves of rCDs and BS12, suggesting the successful formation of Q-rCDs (Fig. S7C). Fig. S7D shows that the fluorescence emission properties of Q-rCDs had no obvious change compared with rCDs. In addition, XPS results also demonstrated the structural composition of Q-rCDs as depicted in Fig. S8. Briefly, Fig. S8A showed three peaks of C1s at 285.00 eV, N1s at 400.00 eV, and O1s at 529.00 eV, indicating that Q-rCDs were mainly composed of C (62.76 %), N (13.87 %), and O (23.37 %). The high resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, and O1s were illustrated by the peak-differentiating and imitating with its corresponding groups, as shown in Fig. S8 B–D. In addition, Fig. S9 showed the solution of rCDs and Q-rCDs had good fluorescent stability within 10 days.

Inspired by the effective antibacterial activity of quaternized CDs with blue-green emission [49,50], the rCDs prepared in this work were successfully functionalized by BS12 and the antibacterial activity against two representative bacterial strains (S. aureus and E. coli) was evaluated by the plate counting method. As shown in Fig. 8 A, the proliferation of S. aureus was remarkably inhibited in the presence of Q-rCDs with low concentration (12.5 μg/mL), and no bacterial colony was observed on the LB agar plates after treatment with 150 μg/mL Q-rCDs. The values of median inhibitory concentration (IC50) and MIC were 25.51 and 150 μg/mL, respectively. For Gram negative bacterium E. coli, the IC50 and MIC of Q-rCDs were 98.24 and 500 μg/mL, respectively (Fig. 8B). These results demonstrated that Q-rCDs had significant inhibitory abilities for bacteria, especially for S. aureus. In addition, the IC50 and MIC of BS12 for S. aureus were 18.77 and 100 μg/mL, respectively, whereas the IC50 and MIC of BS12 for E. coli were 100.5 and 250 μg/mL, respectively (Fig. S10). In a word, the antibacterial performance of Q-rCDs was slightly weaker than that of BS12 against S. aureus, indicating that the Q-rCD could both retain the favorable optical performance of bare rCDs and gain the antiseptic properties of BS12. Furthermore, Fig. S11 shows that the zeta potential of Q-rCDs was 6.96 mV on account of quaternary ammonium groups on the surface of Q-rCDs, which may contribute to the excellent antibacterial ability of Q-rCDs [49,51]. According to previous literature [52,53], the surface potentials of both E. coli and S. aureus are negative and can easily bond to the CDs with positive charges through electrostatic interaction, which may damage the bacterial cell membranes [54]. It is currently recognized as a major mechanism of CDs killing bacteria [55–57]. Hence, we preliminarily proposed that the antibacterial ability of Q-rCDs may be the result of electrostatic interaction which would alter the permeability of the bacterial membrane and ultimately lead to bacterial death.

In conclusion, we reported a green and facile one-step synthesis of red-emissive CDs with high QY. The rCDs possessed small particle sizes, favorable optical properties, and excellent water solubility, which was demonstrated by a series of characterization methods. Then, the cytotoxicity test and acute toxicity test in vivo showed that as-prepared rCDs had minimal toxicity and good biocompatibility, which provided the basis for follow-up bioimaging using rCDs as the fluorescent probe in A549 cells, zebrafish, and nude mice. Moreover, the as-prepared rCDs have been designed as a ratiometric sensing platform for determining curcumin. The result was satisfactory, which showed that the sensing system had good sensitivity, high selectivity, and effective feasibility. In addition, the Q-rCDs exerted an antibacterial effect against Gram positive (S. aureus) and Gram negative bacteria (E. coli) when the original rCDs were modified with BS12, which provided great potential as alternatives to antibiotics. Therefore, the multifunctional rCDs prepared without any organic reagent were prospective long-wavelength nanomaterials for biological imaging, fluorescence sensing, and antibacterial materials.

Jiao Chen: Project administration, Writing – original draft. Ting Li: Investigation. Chengzhang Lin: Software. Yongxing Hou: Formal analysis. Shuanghuai Cheng: Supervision. Bingmiao Gao: Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Hainan Provincial Key Point Research and Invention Program (no. ZDYF2022SHFZ309), Natural Science Foundation of Hainan Province For High-level Talents Project (821RC568) and a Special scientific research project of the Hainan academician innovation platform (no. YSPTZX202132). Moreover, the authors are grateful for the support and assistance in terms of zebrafish, instruments and facilities provided by the Public Research Center of Hainan Medical University, as well as the help of Shuanghuai Cheng in the operation of transmission electron microscopy for ultrastructure photography.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.125458.
